questions.question: What is this course about?
questions.answer: This course teaches you how to write Python programs that control real hardware. You will blink LEDs, read sensors, spin motors, and even build robots — all using a tiny computer called a microcontroller. The branch of computer science that reads sensors and controls lights and motors is called physical computing. By the end of the course, you will be able to design and build your own projects from scratch. No prior programming or electronics experience is needed to begin.

questions.question: Who is this course for?
questions.answer: This course was first built for students in 5th through 12th grade (ages 10–18), but people of all ages from around the world use it to learn MicroPython. If you have never programmed before and want to make things light up, move, and react to the world, this course is for you. Parents, teachers, and hobbyists are also very welcome.

questions.question: What do I need to get started?
questions.answer: You need three things: a microcontroller (the $4 Raspberry Pi Pico is recommended), a USB Micro-B cable to connect the Pico to your computer, and a few basic parts including LEDs, resistors, a solderless breadboard, and jumper wires. A starter kit costs under $20. You also need a free program called Thonny installed on your computer.

questions.question: What is a microcontroller?
questions.answer: A microcontroller is a tiny computer built into a single chip. It has a processor, memory, and input/output pins all in one small package. Unlike a laptop or phone, a microcontroller does not run a full operating system. It runs one program at a time and uses its pins to talk to the physical world — reading sensors, lighting LEDs, or spinning motors. The Raspberry Pi Pico uses the RP2040 chip and costs $4.

questions.question: What is the Raspberry Pi Pico?
questions.answer: The Raspberry Pi Pico is a small, low-cost microcontroller board made by the Raspberry Pi Foundation. It uses the RP2040 chip and costs about $4. The Pico has 26 GPIO pins you can use to connect sensors, displays, motors, and more. Versions include the basic Pico, the Pico W (adds Wi-Fi), and the Pico 2 (more memory and speed). Most lessons in this course use the Pico or Pico W.

questions.question: What is MicroPython?
questions.answer: MicroPython is a version of Python designed to run on small microcontrollers. It includes most of the features of regular Python, plus special libraries for controlling hardware — reading pins, driving motors, and talking to displays. MicroPython was created by Damien George in 2014 and is now the most popular way to program microcontrollers with Python.

questions.question: How do I set up my computer to use MicroPython?
questions.answer: The easiest setup uses Thonny. Download and install Thonny, connect your Pico via USB, hold the BOOTSEL button while plugging in, then release. In Thonny, go to Tools → Options → Interpreter, choose MicroPython (Raspberry Pi Pico), and click Install or update MicroPython. After these steps you can type code in the Thonny shell and run it on your Pico immediately.

questions.question: What is Thonny?
questions.answer: Thonny is a free, lightweight code editor designed for beginners. It runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux. Thonny lets you write and run MicroPython programs on your Pico, see error messages in plain language, and step through your code one line at a time to find bugs. It is the recommended editor for this course.

questions.question: Can I use an ESP32 instead of a Raspberry Pi Pico?
questions.answer: Yes. The ESP32 is another popular microcontroller that runs MicroPython. It has built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth and typically costs $5–$10. Many lessons in this course work on an ESP32 with small changes. The main difference is that the Pico is simpler and cheaper for beginners, while the ESP32 is better for wireless projects from the start.

questions.question: Do I need to know Python before I start?
questions.answer: No prior Python knowledge is required. The course starts from the very beginning and teaches you the programming concepts you need as you go. If you already know some Python, you will move through the early sections quickly. The course covers variables, loops, functions, and modules — all the Python basics you need.

questions.question: How long does the course take to complete?
questions.answer: Most students can finish the Getting Started section and Basic Examples in a few afternoons. The full course — including sensors, motors, displays, sound, and robots — can take a semester at school or a few months of weekend learning. There is no deadline; you can go at your own pace and skip to sections that interest you most.

questions.question: Where can I ask questions or get help?
questions.answer: The best place is the GitHub Discussions page at github.com/dmccreary/learning-micropython/discussions. You can also use the search function at the top of this website. If you find a bug or want to suggest a topic, open a GitHub Issue or see the Contributing Guide.

questions.question: What is physical computing?
questions.answer: Physical computing means writing programs that interact with the real, physical world. Instead of showing text on a screen, a physical computing program might turn on a light when it gets dark, play a sound when a button is pressed, or stop a robot when it detects an obstacle. Physical computing connects software (your code) and hardware (wires, sensors, motors). MicroPython is one of the best tools for learning it.

questions.question: What is a GPIO pin?
questions.answer: GPIO stands for General-Purpose Input/Output. These are the small metal pins on the side of your Pico. You can set them as outputs (send a signal to turn on an LED or motor) or inputs (read a signal from a button or sensor). The Raspberry Pi Pico has 26 GPIO pins, numbered GP0 through GP28. In MicroPython code, you always use the GP number, not the physical pin number on the board.

questions.question: What is a breadboard?
questions.answer: A breadboard is a plastic board with lots of small holes that let you connect electronic parts without soldering. The holes are wired together in rows and columns underneath, so you can connect LEDs, resistors, and sensors by pushing their legs into the right holes. Breadboards make it easy to try circuits, move parts around, and fix mistakes — perfect for learning. All labs in this course use a solderless breadboard.

questions.question: What is PWM?
questions.answer: PWM stands for Pulse-Width Modulation. It is a way to control the average power going to a device by switching it on and off very quickly. The percentage of time it is on is called the duty cycle. A 100% duty cycle means full brightness or full speed. A 50% duty cycle means half. MicroPython uses the machine.PWM class to create PWM signals for fading LEDs, controlling motor speed, and moving servo motors.

questions.question: What is I2C?
questions.answer: I2C (pronounced I-squared-C) stands for Inter-Integrated Circuit. It is a simple two-wire communication protocol that lets your microcontroller talk to sensors and displays using just an SDA (data) wire and an SCL (clock) wire. You can connect many devices to the same two wires because each device has a unique I2C address. MicroPython uses the machine.I2C class to communicate over I2C.

questions.question: What is an ADC?
questions.answer: ADC stands for Analog-to-Digital Converter. Most of the real world is analog — light, temperature, and sound all change smoothly. But microcontrollers only understand digital numbers. An ADC measures an analog voltage and converts it to a number your program can use. The Raspberry Pi Pico has three ADC inputs on pins GP26, GP27, and GP28, each measuring 0–3.3 V and returning a number from 0 to 65535. MicroPython uses the machine.ADC class to read these values.

questions.question: What is the REPL?
questions.answer: REPL stands for Read-Evaluate-Print Loop. It is the interactive shell at the bottom of Thonny. You type a line of Python, press Enter, and the Pico runs it immediately and shows the result. The REPL is great for experimenting. You can test a single line of code without writing a full program.

questions.question: What is a loop in MicroPython?
questions.answer: A loop is a block of code that runs over and over. In MicroPython programs for hardware, the most common loop is while True: — this runs forever until you stop the program or the Pico loses power. A for loop runs a fixed number of times, like when you want to light up each NeoPixel one by one. Loops are how your program keeps reading sensors and reacting to the world continuously.

questions.question: What is debouncing?
questions.answer: When you press a button, the metal contacts inside bounce very quickly before settling into the pressed position. This can make your program think many buttons were pressed instead of just one. Debouncing is the fix. Software debouncing adds a small delay after detecting a press before checking again, ignoring the bounce noise. Hardware debouncing uses a capacitor to smooth the signal.

questions.question: What is a NeoPixel?
questions.answer: A NeoPixel is a small, full-color LED you can control with a single wire from your microcontroller. Each NeoPixel contains red, green, and blue LEDs so you can set it to any color by mixing those three values. NeoPixels are also called WS2812B LEDs. You can connect many in a strip or ring and control each one individually. MicroPython has a built-in neopixel module to make this easy.

questions.question: What is a servo motor?
questions.answer: A servo motor is a special motor that turns to a specific angle and holds it there. Standard servos turn between 0° and 180°. A continuous rotation servo spins like a regular motor but with speed control. You control a servo with a PWM signal at 50 Hz — the length of each pulse tells the servo what angle to reach. MicroPython uses the machine.PWM class to generate this signal.

questions.question: What is computational thinking?
questions.answer: Computational thinking is a way of solving problems using four key steps: decomposition (break a big problem into smaller pieces), pattern recognition (find similarities to problems you have already solved), abstraction (focus on the important parts and ignore the rest), and algorithms (write a step-by-step plan). Every MicroPython project in this course uses computational thinking. When you build a robot, you decompose it into power, motors, sensors, and control logic — then solve each piece.

questions.question: What is a sensor?
questions.answer: A sensor is a device that measures something in the physical world and converts it into an electrical signal your microcontroller can read. Common sensors include the DHT11 for temperature and humidity, HC-SR04 for distance using sound, VL53L0X for laser distance, APDS9960 for gesture and color, and photoresistors for light level. Each sensor has its own way of connecting — many use I2C or simple analog/digital signals.

questions.question: What is a function in MicroPython?
questions.answer: A function is a reusable block of code that you give a name. Instead of writing the same code over and over, you define a function once and call it whenever you need it. Functions make your programs shorter, easier to read, and easier to fix. You define a function with the def keyword and call it by writing its name followed by parentheses.

questions.question: What is a module in MicroPython?
questions.answer: A module is a file of Python code you can use in other programs by importing it. MicroPython has many built-in modules: machine for hardware control, utime for timing, neopixel for LED strips, and network for Wi-Fi (Pico W). You load a module with the import statement. You can also install extra driver modules to support specific sensors and displays.

questions.question: What is the RP2040 chip?
questions.answer: The RP2040 is a custom microcontroller chip designed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation that powers the Raspberry Pi Pico. It has a dual-core ARM Cortex-M0+ processor running up to 133 MHz, 264 KB of SRAM, 2 MB of flash, 26 GPIO pins, three 12-bit ADC inputs, and 8 programmable I/O state machines. The 264 KB of RAM makes it ideal for display projects that older microcontrollers like the Arduino Uno (2 KB) could not handle.

questions.question: What is a UF2 file?
questions.answer: A UF2 file is the file you drag onto the Pico to install MicroPython firmware. When you hold BOOTSEL while plugging in the Pico, it appears as a USB drive called RPI-RP2. Drag the MicroPython UF2 file onto that drive and the Pico installs the firmware and restarts automatically. You only need to do this once unless you update MicroPython to a newer version.

questions.question: What is the machine module?
questions.answer: The machine module is MicroPython's main hardware control library. It provides machine.Pin for GPIO control, machine.PWM for PWM signals, machine.ADC for reading analog voltages, machine.I2C for I2C communication, and machine.SPI for SPI communication. Almost every MicroPython hardware program starts with from machine import Pin or a similar import.

questions.question: What does Pin.OUT mean?
questions.answer: When you set up a GPIO pin, you tell MicroPython whether it is an output or input. Pin.OUT means the Pico sends a signal out through that pin — use this for LEDs, buzzers, and motor control. Pin.IN means the Pico reads a signal coming in — use this for buttons and sensors. You set the mode when creating the Pin object: led = Pin(15, Pin.OUT).

questions.question: What is a pull-up resistor?
questions.answer: A pull-up resistor connects a GPIO input pin to 3.3 V through a large resistor (usually 10,000 ohms) so the pin reads HIGH when no button is pressed and LOW when the button connects it to ground. Without a pull-up, the pin floats and gives random readings. The Pico has built-in pull-up resistors you can enable in software with Pin.PULL_UP — no extra hardware needed.

questions.question: What is an interrupt?
questions.answer: An interrupt is a signal that pauses your main program and runs a special function called an interrupt handler immediately. This lets you catch very fast events — like a quick button press — that your main loop might miss while doing other work. In MicroPython you set up an interrupt with the .irq() method on a Pin: button.irq(trigger=Pin.IRQ_FALLING, handler=my_function).

questions.question: What is a framebuffer?
questions.answer: A framebuffer is a section of RAM that stores a picture of what should be shown on your display. When you draw text, lines, or shapes in MicroPython, you update the framebuffer first. Then you call .show() to send the entire framebuffer to the display at once, making the update look smooth. For a 128x64 monochrome OLED, the framebuffer takes 1 KB of RAM.

questions.question: What is an I2C address?
questions.answer: Every device on an I2C bus has a unique address — a number that identifies it. When the Pico wants to talk to a specific sensor or display, it sends that device's address first so only that device listens. Common addresses include 0x3C for most SSD1306 OLED displays and 0x29 for the VL53L0X time-of-flight sensor. Run an I2C scanner to find the address of a device you are not sure about.

questions.question: My LED does not light up. What do I check?
questions.answer: Work through this checklist: Check LED polarity (longer leg to GPIO, shorter to GND). Make sure you are using a current-limiting resistor (330 ohms typical). Confirm the pin number in your code matches the GP number you wired to, not the physical pin number. Check the Thonny shell for error messages. Confirm your Pico is connected and Thonny is using the MicroPython interpreter.

questions.question: I see an ImportError. What does that mean?
questions.answer: An ImportError means MicroPython cannot find the module you are importing. Either the module name is spelled wrong (Python is case-sensitive), or the driver file has not been copied to the Pico. For example, ssd1306.py must be on the Pico before you can import ssd1306. Use the Thonny file manager or mpremote to copy driver files.

questions.question: My sensor gives wrong readings. What do I check?
questions.answer: Sensor problems are usually wiring, wrong I2C pins, or a missing driver. Check that VCC, GND, SDA, and SCL match the wiring diagram — swapping SDA and SCL is a common mistake. Confirm the I2C pins in your code match the physical pins you wired. If the sensor needs a driver file (like bme280.py), make sure it is copied to the Pico. Run an I2C scanner to confirm the device is detected.

questions.question: My display shows nothing. What do I check?
questions.answer: For a blank OLED or LCD: confirm 3.3V and GND are connected. Check that SDA and SCL are on the correct pins. The default I2C address in most drivers is 0x3C but your display may use 0x3D — run an I2C scanner to find the real address. Most importantly, make sure your code calls oled.show() after drawing — nothing appears on screen until you do.

questions.question: My Pico will not connect to my computer. What do I do?
questions.answer: Try these steps in order: use a different USB cable (many cables are charge-only with no data wires), try a different USB port, reinstall firmware (hold BOOTSEL, plug in, drag UF2 file), and restart Thonny. If the Pico is stuck in a crash loop from a broken program, press the Stop button in Thonny right after connecting to interrupt it.

questions.question: I get an IndentationError. What does that mean?
questions.answer: Python uses indentation to show what code is inside a loop or function. An IndentationError means the indentation is inconsistent — usually because you mixed tabs and spaces. In Thonny, go to Options → Editor and turn on Use spaces instead of tabs. Always use 4 spaces per indentation level. All lines inside a while or for loop must be indented by at least 4 spaces.

questions.question: My motor only runs in one direction. What do I check?
questions.answer: DC motors need an H-bridge circuit to run in both directions. The H-bridge reverses the current through the motor. Common H-bridge chips used in this course are the L293D and DRV8833. Check that both motor control pins are connected to the H-bridge, your code is setting both pins (not just one), and the H-bridge is powered correctly — some need 5V for the motor side and 3.3V for the logic side.

questions.question: How do I organize my MicroPython project files?
questions.answer: A clean project structure puts main.py as your main program, config.py for pin numbers and settings, and any driver files (like ssd1306.py or bme280.py) in the same folder. This follows the abstraction principle of computational thinking: hardware details stay in config.py, logic goes in main.py, and drivers handle sensor communication. Changing a wiring connection means updating only config.py.

questions.question: How do I install driver libraries on my Pico?
questions.answer: Driver libraries are .py files you copy onto the Pico. Use the Thonny file manager (open the Files panel, find the file on your computer, right-click, Upload to /) or use mpremote from the command line: mpremote cp ssd1306.py :ssd1306.py. After copying, import the driver in your program just like any other module.

questions.question: How do I save my program so it runs automatically when the Pico powers on?
questions.answer: Save your program as main.py on the Pico. When the Pico powers on, it automatically runs main.py. If you also have a boot.py file, it runs first — use boot.py for setup tasks like connecting to Wi-Fi. In Thonny, save using File → Save As and choose the MicroPython device, not your local computer.

questions.question: How do I connect my Pico W to Wi-Fi?
questions.answer: The Pico W has a built-in Wi-Fi chip. Use the network module to connect: import network, create a WLAN object, call wlan.active(True), then wlan.connect(ssid, password). Wait in a loop until wlan.isconnected() returns True, then use wlan.ifconfig()[0] to get your IP address. Once connected you can make HTTP requests or build a web server.
